title: IBM smoothing with INLA
code: library(ibmsmooth)  set.seed(1) t <- sort(runif(40, 0, 10)) y <- sin(t) + rnorm(length(t), 0, 0.25)  fit_inla <- ibm(   t = t,   y = y,   method = "inla",   adaptive = FALSE,   initial_sd = 5 )  plots <- plot(fit_inla) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot summary(fit_inla)

title: IBM smoothing with INLA
text: precision curve evaluated transition midpoints. High process precision corresponds stronger local smoothing; low process precision corresponds locally variable derivative process. plotting helper uses posterior samples get_hyperparameter_samples() attaches summarized plotting data returned object.
code: t <- sort(runif(40, 0, 10)) y <- rnorm(length(t), 0, 0.25) y <- y + ifelse(t < 5, 0, 2)  fit_inla <- ibm(   t = t,   y = y,   method = "inla",   adaptive = FALSE,   initial_sd = 1 )  plots <- plot(fit_inla) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot summary(fit_inla)  fit_inla_adapt <- ibm(   t = t,   y = y,   method = "inla",   adaptive = TRUE,   n_knots = 10,   adaptive_deviation_sd = 1.5,   initial_sd = 1 )  plots <- plot(fit_inla_adapt) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot  precision_plot <- plot_precision_curve(fit_inla_adapt, n_samples = 1000) precision_plot precision_summary <- attr(precision_plot, "precision_summary") head(precision_summary)

title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: small problem, exported validation helper independently constructs dense observation covariance compares multivariate Gaussian log likelihood Kalman-filter result. difference close machine precision.
code: set.seed(4) t_check <- sort(runif(8)) y_check <- rnorm(8) tau_check <- exp(rnorm(7, -2, 0.3))  validate_marginalized_horseshoe(   t_check, y_check, sigma = 0.25, tau = tau_check ) ##        kalman         dense    difference  ## -5.660236e+01 -5.660236e+01  4.646949e-12

title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: centered, noncentered, marginalized horseshoe models now use correlated unit-time IBM prior: (f1f1′)∼N{0,𝚒𝚗𝚒𝚝𝚒𝚊𝚕_𝚜𝚍2(1/31/21/21)}. \begin{pmatrix}f_1\\f'_1\end{pmatrix} \sim N\left\{0,\ \texttt{initial\_sd}^2 \begin{pmatrix}1/3&1/2\\1/2&1\end{pmatrix}\right\}. also convention used INLA implementation. Standardizing prior removes earlier difference centered noncentered Stan files makes posterior comparison meaningful.

title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
code: set.seed(11) n <- 80 t <- sort(runif(n, 0, 10)) truth <- sin(t) + 0.7 * plogis(12 * (t - 5)) y <- truth + rnorm(n, 0, 0.2)

what: Prepare the same scaled data used by ibm_smooth()
title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: following helper local vignette prepares data low-level Stan comparison rather ordinary package interface.
code: make_horseshoe_stan_data <- function(t, y, infer_at = NULL,                                      log_sigma = list(mu = -1, sd = 1),                                      zeta = 0.1, initial_sd = 5) {   stopifnot(length(t) == length(y), length(unique(t)) >= 2)    grid_raw <- sort(unique(c(t, infer_at)))   dt_mean <- mean(diff(grid_raw))   grid <- (grid_raw - min(grid_raw)) / dt_mean   t_scaled <- (t - min(grid_raw)) / dt_mean   ord <- order(match(t_scaled, grid))    list(     N_obs = length(y),     T = length(grid),     obs_time_idx = match(t_scaled, grid)[ord],     deltat = diff(grid),     y_obs = as.numeric(scale(y))[ord],     regular = as.integer(all(abs(diff(grid) - diff(grid)[1]) <                                sqrt(.Machine$double.eps))),     log_sigma_mu = log_sigma$mu,     log_sigma_sd = log_sigma$sd,     zeta = zeta,     initial_sd = initial_sd   ) }  stan_data <- make_horseshoe_stan_data(t, y)

title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: chunks evaluated building package vignette compiling sampling two Stan models expensive. Run interactively package source directory. ordinary marginalized fit reconstructed curve draws, use package wrapper instead compiling model manually: also available explicit opt-engine unified interface. existing joint sampler remains default.
code: library(rstan) rstan_options(auto_write = TRUE) options(mc.cores = parallel::detectCores())  existing_file <- system.file(   "stan", "ibm_adaptive_horseshoe.stan", package = "ibmsmooth" ) marginal_file <- system.file(   "stan", "ibm_adaptive_horseshoe_gaussian_marginalized.stan",   package = "ibmsmooth" )  # When running directly from a source checkout rather than an installed package: if (!nzchar(existing_file))   existing_file <- "inst/stan/ibm_adaptive_horseshoe.stan" if (!nzchar(marginal_file))   marginal_file <- "inst/stan/ibm_adaptive_horseshoe_gaussian_marginalized.stan"  existing_model <- stan_model(existing_file) marginal_model <- stan_model(marginal_file)  set.seed(2026) existing_time <- system.time({   existing_fit <- sampling(     existing_model, data = stan_data,     chains = 4, iter = 2000, seed = 2026,     control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95, max_treedepth = 12)   ) })  set.seed(2026) marginal_time <- system.time({   marginal_fit <- sampling(     marginal_model,     data = stan_data[names(stan_data) != "regular"],     chains = 4, iter = 2000, seed = 2026,     control = list(adapt_delta = 0.95, max_treedepth = 12)   ) }) fit_marginalized <- ibm_horseshoe_marginalized(   t, y,   n_state_draws = 1000,   chains = 4,   iter = 2000,   seed = 2026,   adapt_delta = 0.95 )  plots <- plot(fit_marginalized) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot  f_draws <- get_samples(fit_marginalized, "f") fprime_draws <- get_samples(fit_marginalized, "fprime") fit_marginalized <- ibm(   t, y,   method = "stan",   adaptive = TRUE,   stan_adaptive_method = "horseshoe",   stan_horseshoe_engine = "marginalized",   n_state_draws = 1000,   chains = 4,   iter = 2000,   seed = 2026,   adapt_delta = 0.95 )

what: Compare posterior agreement and sampling efficiency
title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: hyperparameter names shared two models. Agreement sigma, gamma, representative tau values checks target distribution; effective sample size per elapsed second measures whether marginalization actually useful. Also inspect divergences rather relying runtime alone: prototype promising posterior summaries agree, diagnostics deteriorate, effective sample size per second improves datasets size package meant handle. Marginalization reduces sampled dimension, differentiating Kalman recursion every leapfrog step cost, elapsed time sufficient comparison.
code: pars <- c("sigma", "gamma", "tau[1]", paste0("tau[", stan_data$T - 1, "]"))  posterior_table <- function(fit, label) {   s <- summary(fit, pars = pars)$summary   data.frame(     model = label,     parameter = rownames(s),     mean = s[, "mean"],     sd = s[, "sd"],     n_eff = s[, "n_eff"],     Rhat = s[, "Rhat"],     row.names = NULL   ) }  comparison <- rbind(   posterior_table(existing_fit, "existing noncentered"),   posterior_table(marginal_fit, "marginalized") ) comparison  elapsed <- c(   existing = unname(existing_time["elapsed"]),   marginalized = unname(marginal_time["elapsed"]) ) elapsed  aggregate(n_eff ~ model, comparison, sum) count_divergences <- function(fit) {   sum(vapply(     get_sampler_params(fit, inc_warmup = FALSE),     function(x) sum(x[, "divergent__"]),     numeric(1)   )) }  c(   existing = count_divergences(existing_fit),   marginalized = count_divergences(marginal_fit) )

title: Prototype: marginalized Gaussian horseshoe IBM
text: first result can unusually favorable unfavorable. following scenarios exercise globally smooth curve, sharp change, locally varying roughness several grid sizes. Keep data-generating seed distinct Stan seed experiment can repeated systematically. simulation smoother tested corresponding dense conditional Gaussian posterior, including case replicated observations grid points introduced infer_at. separates correctness state reconstruction HMC performance.
code: simulate_scenario <- function(n, scenario, seed) {   set.seed(seed)   t <- sort(runif(n, 0, 10))   truth <- switch(     scenario,     smooth = sin(t),     jump = sin(t) + ifelse(t >= 5, 1, 0),     varying = sin(t) + ifelse(t > 4 & t < 7, 0.3 * sin(12 * t), 0)   )   list(t = t, y = truth + rnorm(n, 0, 0.2), truth = truth) }  benchmark_grid <- expand.grid(   n = c(30, 100, 300),   scenario = c("smooth", "jump", "varying"),   data_seed = 1:3,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE )  # Use the fitting and diagnostic code above inside a loop over benchmark_grid. # Save one row per fit containing elapsed time, divergences, maximum R-hat, and # bulk/tail ESS per second. Curve RMSE and interval coverage can be computed # from get_samples() for the marginalized wrapper and the existing ibmfit.

title: IBM smoothing with Stan
code: library(ibmsmooth)  set.seed(1) t <- sort(runif(40, 0, 10)) y <- sin(t) + rnorm(length(t), 0, 0.25)  fit <- ibm(t, y, method = "stan", adaptive = FALSE, chains = 2, iter = 1000,            initial_sd = 5)  plots <- plot(fit) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot  summary(fit)

what: Locally adaptive IBM with a horseshoe prior

code: fit_hs <- ibm(   t = t,   y = y,   method = "stan",   adaptive = TRUE,   stan_adaptive_method = "horseshoe",   chains = 2,   iter = 1000,   initial_sd = 5 )  plots <- plot(fit_hs) plots$function_plot plots$derivative_plot  plot_precision_curve(fit_hs) summary(fit_hs)

what: Unified interface for integrated Brownian motion smoothing — ibm

title: Unified interface for integrated Brownian motion smoothing — ibm
code: ibm(   t,   y,   infer_at = NULL,   method = c("stan", "inla"),   adaptive = FALSE,   stan_adaptive_method = c("horseshoe", "baseline_horseshoe", "rw", "rhs", "bridge"),   stan_horseshoe_engine = c("joint", "marginalized"),   ... )

title: Unified interface for integrated Brownian motion smoothing — ibm
text: t Numeric vector time points. y Numeric vector observations times t. infer_at Optional numeric vector additional time points inference returned. method Computational backend, either "stan" "inla". adaptive Logical. FALSE, use single smoothness parameter. TRUE, fit locally adaptive model. stan_adaptive_method Stan-adaptive prior. default "horseshoe". Use "baseline_horseshoe" independent horseshoe excess roughness positive global IBM baseline. stan_horseshoe_engine Stan engine used adaptive horseshoe model. "joint" retains existing sampler; "marginalized" uses Gaussian-data Kalman marginalization simulation smoother. ... Additional arguments passed ibm_smooth() ibm_inla_fit().

title: Unified interface for integrated Brownian motion smoothing — ibm
text: object class ibmfit.

what: Fit an integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA — ibm_inla_fit
title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA — ibm_inla_fit
text: Fit global locally adaptive integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA. latent field state vector (f, fprime) latent time grid. observation model Gaussian.

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA — ibm_inla_fit
code: ibm_inla_fit(   t,   y,   infer_at = NULL,   adaptive = FALSE,   n_knots = 5L,   bs_degree = 3L,   adaptive_deviation_sd = 0.5,   log_process_sd = list(mu = -2, sd = 0.5),   log_sigma = list(mu = -1, sd = 1),   theta_mu = NULL,   theta_sd = NULL,   sigma_mu = NULL,   sigma_sd = NULL,   initial_sd = 5,   initial_slope_sd = NULL,   control.compute = list(config = TRUE),   ... )

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA — ibm_inla_fit
text: t Numeric vector time points. y Numeric vector observations times t. infer_at Optional numeric vector additional time points inference returned. adaptive Logical. FALSE, use one global diffusion-precision parameter. TRUE, model log diffusion precision global log precision plus centered B-spline deviations shrunk toward zero. n_knots Number internal knots B-spline basis adaptive = TRUE. bs_degree Degree B-spline basis. adaptive_deviation_sd Prior standard deviation centered B-spline deviation coefficients adaptive INLA fits. adaptive model uses log(lambda_i) = alpha + B_i beta, alpha global log diffusion precision components beta independent N(0, adaptive_deviation_sd^2) priors. Smaller values shrink model strongly toward global-precision IBM. log_process_sd List elements mu sd giving prior mean standard deviation log IBM process standard deviation scaled model scale. default matches Stan backend: list(mu = -2, sd = 0.5). log_sigma List elements mu sd giving prior mean standard deviation log Gaussian observation standard deviation scaled model scale. default matches Stan backend: list(mu = -1, sd = 1). theta_mu Optional advanced override mean internal INLA global log diffusion-precision parameter. NULL, set log_process_sd. theta_sd Optional advanced override standard deviation internal INLA global log diffusion-precision parameter. NULL, set log_process_sd. sigma_mu Optional advanced override mean Gaussian likelihood log precision. NULL, set log_sigma. sigma_sd Optional advanced override standard deviation Gaussian likelihood log precision. NULL, set log_sigma. initial_sd Prior scale joint initial state c(f_1, fprime_1). initial covariance initial_sd^2 * matrix(c(1/3, 1/2, 1/2, 1), 2, 2), unit-time integrated Brownian motion covariance level derivative. initial_slope_sd Deprecated. Retained backward compatibility; ignored INLA backend. control.compute List passed INLA::inla() control.compute. Defaults list(config = TRUE) posterior sampling works plotting, summaries, get_samples_inla(). ... Additional arguments passed INLA::inla().

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion smoother using INLA — ibm_inla_fit
text: object class ibmfit.

what: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
text: Fit Bayesian integrated Brownian motion smoothing model observations (t, y) using Stan. function rescales time response efficient sampling, constructs data list Stan, runs rstan::stan() centered non-centered Stan implementation.

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
code: ibm_smooth(   t = NULL,   y = NULL,   infer_at = NULL,   get_code = FALSE,   write_to_file = NULL,   adaptive = "nonadaptive",   log_sigma = list(mu = -1, sd = 1),   log_tau = list(mu = -2, sd = 0.5),   zeta = 0.1,   slab_scale = 2,   alpha = 0.5,   initial_sd = 5,   centered = NULL,   noncentered = NULL,   iter = 2000,   chains = 4,   cores = getOption("mc.cores", chains),   max_treedepth = 12,   adapt_delta = 0.9,   ... )

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
text: t Numeric vector time points. y Numeric vector observations times t. Must length t. infer_at Optional numeric vector time points function inference (addition just observed time points). NULL included Stan data fitted function returned points well. get_code Logical; TRUE return Stan model code single string (potentially writing write_to_file). get_code = TRUE t y NULL function return Stan code without attempting fit model. write_to_file Optional character path. provided get_code = TRUE Stan code written file. adaptive Character; specifies use adaptive smoothing. Options "nonadaptive" (default), "rw", "horseshoe", "baseline_horseshoe", "rhs", "bridge". log_sigma List elements mu sd giving prior mean standard deviation log(sigma). Defaults list(mu = -1, sd = 1). log_tau List elements mu sd giving prior mean standard deviation log(tau). Used adaptive = "nonadaptive", "rw", "baseline_horseshoe". latter prior positive baseline process scale tau0. Defaults list(mu = -2, sd = 0.5). zeta Numeric; global shrinkage scale used horseshoe, baseline_horseshoe, rhs bridge priors. baseline-horseshoe model controls excess roughness. Default 0.1. slab_scale Numeric; slab scale regularized horseshoe (rhs). Default 2. alpha Numeric (0,2]; exponent bridge prior (used adaptive = "bridge"). Default 0.5. initial_sd Prior scale correlated initial state. Stan backend now uses unit-time IBM initial covariance INLA backend: c(f_1, fprime_1), covariance initial_sd^2 * matrix(c(1/3, 1/2, 1/2, 1), 2, 2) scaled response/time scale. centered Logical; TRUE use centered Stan parameterization. NULL sensible default chosen: centered TRUE adaptive == "nonadaptive" (except "bridge" forces centered = TRUE). can alternatively pass noncentered (logical) explicitly request non-centered form. noncentered Deprecated alias specifying non-centered parameterization. iter Integer; total number iterations chain (including warmup). Default 2000. chains Integer; number MCMC chains. Default 4. cores Integer; number parallel processes use Stan chains. Defaults getOption("mc.cores", chains). max_treedepth Integer; maximum tree depth Stan's NUTS sampler. Default 12. adapt_delta Numeric (0,1); target average acceptance probability Stan's NUTS. Default 0.85. ... Additional arguments passed rstan::stan().

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
text: object class ibmfit (list) components: stanfit stanfit object returned rstan::stan(). data list containing original data scaling constants used: y_raw, y_mean, y_sd, t_raw, t_min, dt_mean.

title: Fit an integrated Brownian motion (IBM) smoother using Stan — ibm_smooth
text: function supports several adaptive smoothing options: "nonadaptive" (default): single global smoothing parameter (tau). "rw": random-walk prior log(tau) across segments. "horseshoe": horseshoe prior local IBM process scales. "baseline_horseshoe": horseshoe excess roughness positive IBM baseline. "rhs": regularized horseshoe prior local IBM process scales. "bridge": bridge prior whitened IBM transition innovations (requires centered parameterization). function rescales y zero mean unit variance rescales time mean time step near 1. flag regular computed internally indicate whether time points equally spaced. transition covariance interval tau_i^2 S_i, S_i IBM covariance determined deltat[]. INLA uses equivalent precision parameterization lambda_i = 1 / tau_i^2. get_code = TRUE Stan code used chosen combination adaptive centered returned single string; write_to_file provided code also written path.

what: Plot the locally adaptive process precision curve — plot_precision_curve
title: Plot the locally adaptive process precision curve — plot_precision_curve
text: Plot posterior summaries time-varying IBM process precision locally adaptive fit. curve evaluated transition midpoints, plotted time value represents interval two adjacent latent grid points.

title: Plot the locally adaptive process precision curve — plot_precision_curve
code: plot_precision_curve(   ibmfit,   n_samples = 1000,   level = 0.95,   inner_level = 0.8,   log_y = TRUE,   title = NULL,   line_color = "purple4",   ribbon_fill = "purple",   alpha_outer = 0.18,   alpha_inner = 0.3,   ... )

title: Plot the locally adaptive process precision curve — plot_precision_curve
text: ibmfit object class ibmfit locally adaptive Stan INLA fit. n_samples Number posterior samples use. INLA fits drawn INLA::inla.posterior.sample(). Stan fits maximum number MCMC draws keep. level Outer pointwise credible interval level. Defaults 0.95. inner_level Optional inner pointwise credible interval level. Defaults 0.80. Use NULL suppress inner interval. log_y Logical. TRUE, display positive precision values log10 y-axis. title Optional plot title. line_color Color posterior median curve. ribbon_fill Fill color credible interval ribbons. alpha_outer Alpha outer credible interval ribbon. alpha_inner Alpha inner credible interval ribbon. ... Additional arguments, currently unused.

title: Plot the locally adaptive process precision curve — plot_precision_curve
text: ggplot object. data used construct plot attached precision_summary attribute.
